speaker: interviewer
text: --Mr. Sidney Finkel. And this is tape number five. Mr. Finkel, you were saying that the train was rolling into the station in Buchenwald in a dark tunnel. Want to continue from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were disembarked from the train. And I was really happy to-- to be there, because they deloused us. We had showers and some chemical-- DT and T, or some-- and I felt clean. For the first time in many years, I felt clean. And they gave us these flannel, little uniforms. So in that way, I was pleased that I was there. And the place was, after being in Tschenstochau, which was such a shambles, this was an organized place. And they took us to a barrack, and-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they, who are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- the whole internal organization was the inmates. There were prisoners there, political prisoners. Some were German. Some were from Belgium and other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a huge place, over 100,000 people. And it was jam packed, because there were-- see, at that time, all the Polish camps had been overrun. So they didn't have that many facilities. So they jammed prisoners into-- prisoners? I wasn't a prisoner. But they jammed us into Buchenwald. So it was very jam packed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were in-- I was in a barrack. And we slept, like, you know, 20, 30-- it was just one big board every few feet. And people slept. And you woke up in the morning, and there were one or two bodies that were dead. And in the morning, this detail would come in, of inmates. They would take all the dead bodies and take them to the crematorium. And things were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you by yourself at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there strictly by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know what happened to your brother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. I had no idea what happened to my brother or my father. And I didn't like it one bit to be there. It was very tough. We had to stay on-- in this cold weather, you had to stay. Each morning, you had to stay on this roll call. And it was really-- sanitary conditions were miserable, but there was some kind of an order there. It was-- I was-- didn't feel like imminent danger or any-- or-- or anything. The food was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was-- I was very fortunate. There was an organization there-- I think they were mostly communists and socialists-- that made it a mission of theirs to save the young people, because the future, you know, was-- we always want to save the children. So they-- they found me, and probably some others, and they took us to a different section of Buchenwald. It was a-- a smaller place that was much, much better. It was far more comfortable. We had more room to sleep in. And we got a little bit better food. And we were supervised by adults. We didn't have to fight the adults, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And you were asking me before about-- also this-- this same committee was instrumental in people who are in-- in the-- this Jewish government working for the Germans where-- where they-- they were there. And there was a committee there-- I don't know, we would call it a justice committee, or whatever-- that what they did was the people that collaborated with the Germans, they would strangle them in the night. They would hold like a meeting, a court, and something like that. And somebody would tell about them. And then, at nighttime, they would come in and strangle them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see that going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I know that the president of our Jewish council-- Weistoff, or something like that-- he saw me there and he was very happy so to see me from his home town, and everything else. And I was kind of cool towards the whole thing. And I did not take place in it, but I knew that they were discussing that. And I knew that some of the bigger people went out and did that. And I think he was one of the-- one of the person that was murdered, or whatever you want to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was murdered as part of the revenge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Retribution. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: The punishment column? Does that ring a bell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Revenge column?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kind of. Yeah, it-- it rings kind of a bell to me. But I was in the same place, in the same hut as this was going on, with the people who did this. Obviously, I was too young to participate in it, you know? But I think I was asked about them, if I knew them, and what they did, and so forth. And I was-- if I remember correctly, I was kind of neutral in the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, in-- in Buchenwald, I happened to see-- one day, I happened to see my father. And he was overjoyed to see me. And he had, I think, some bread or something, the he gave to me. But you know what? I only was with him a few minutes. And then I-- I decided I didn't want to see him. You know? He was-- he looked very bad. He was powerless. And I just felt like I-- I couldn't stand around him. So I went back to this-- my little place with all the other young people. How long were you with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father? Maybe about five or 10 minutes. I just tried to make an excuse to get away. I just didn't want any part of him, because he was helpless. He couldn't help me anymore, and I just needed to be by myself to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what he said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you said to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember saying goodbye to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, just walking away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's important that I tell that, because that's-- that's what happened. And then, what happened-- this was very close-- now, we're going into the spring, probably March or so. And the camp was liberated in April, I think, 11. And the Germans wanted all the Jews out of there. Before, they didn't make a distinction between Jews and non-Jews. Lived in the same blocks and things. But now, they did. And they made us stand in-- you know, in this appellplatz. And they could always tell we were Jews, by-- because they would have us show them that-- our penis and see that we are circumcised. And so, me and a whole bunch of other kids were driven into another camp. It was a smaller camp within the camp. It was a very small compound, surrounded by barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Within Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was in Buchenwald. It was just a building, really, just a-- one building, like a gymnasium, or something. And in that-- right in there, I meet my uncle Rolnig and my two teenage cousins. And again, I'm pleased to see them, but I'm kind of still offish, you know? The-- there's my uncle and his two sons, right by-- by them. And I don't know. I get-- I get this real bad-- bad feelings about being in that place. I want to get away. And I watch people go out. And the guards wouldn't say anything. They just walk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- either I, or this guy, came to me. He says, let's take this garbage pail and get out of here. So I was frightened, but I-- you know, I picked it up, and I moved out, moved out of this little building. What? You mean, you Pretended you were working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: By picking up the garbage can?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And this was days, really, days before the-- the-- the Americans came. And a lot of the guards looked the other way, you know? They-- they-- they just didn't care anymore. But one of the other--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did-- did you know that the Americans were close?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard gunfire, yes. We heard gunfire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew the end-- did you sense the end of the war was near? Did you know the Germans were losing at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because they used to broadcast the news, the German news, into the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you heard what was really going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like you say, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't know distinctly. I didn't know that they were a day away, or two days away, or three days away. I didn't know that. But I knew that, from the gunfire, well, they were very, very close, because I could tell it, again, by the attitude of the guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as soon as we left this little building-- because that's was it was, just a compound, a little building-- one of the German guards wasn't that loose. And he took out his revolver and started firing at us. And we dropped the can, and we ran like hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: So matter of fact, the guy that-- that-- I-- him and I, who-- the guy who helped me carry this outside the camp, I met him in-- one year in London, in my brother's apartment. And I'll probably see him again, next month. So it happened, all of these things. Oh, but the-- the significant thing of it is that he told me-- and it was confirmed later on-- that all of these people that were in this compound, including my uncle and my cousins, were taken out to the forest and shot the same night. Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So escaping that, I-- I escaped with my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did he tell you that? At the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he-- no, he told me that--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know, until after.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no, I had no idea then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see your father again, after that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And the other thing is, you know-- and I'm questioning it, because you would-- normally, you see a close relative that you like, that under no price would you leave them, you would stay with them. And this is how other people behave. But was something about me that would not-- had no contact, human contact, in the sense that I wanted to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was just a-- I was-- turned my feelings off completely. And I just was in a survival mode. I just-- my only purpose was to survive. I had no feelings for anything or anybody. And anyway, then, they were still chasing us. And then, finally, I think it was like we were-- we just gave up. We said, to hell with it. Let's see what's-- they want us out so badly out of Buchenwald, let's see what's on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was like 3,000 of us. And the gates of Buchenwald opened up, and we went outside. And this is the first time I've been outside of Buchenwald. And we-- How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would estimate that I was there about five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were walking-- as it turned out, we were walking to a railroad station. I don't know why they couldn't have loaded us there, you know? I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyhow, we were-- we were walking. And the one incident that stays-- stands out in my mind to this day, very strongly, is that whoever couldn't walk-- because people were in such horrible physical shape, that they just were not physically able to walk, even half a mile, or a mile. So whoever could not walk would fall into the side of the road, into a ditch. And there would be a rifle fire. The guards would shoot them. Those were the-- those were the rules, you know? We knew that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this one real episode was of a father and son. And the son had to probably be maybe 18 or 19. And the father maybe was in his 40s. And the father turned to him and said, I cannot walk anymore. And the son would keep urging him to keep walking. He says-- so he would walk for a little while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, he couldn't walk anymore. And the son would continuously urge him, help him to walk. Finally, he just covered his face with this hands, fell into a ditch. And the son is shrieking at him to get up. Please get up. And he wouldn't get up, you see? He was saying the-- a Jewish prayer. Sh'ma Yisra'eil Adonai Eloheinu. And the guard was waiting for him to get up. And the son was shrieking for him to get up. And then, there was shot. And that was it. You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That, I saw. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very much. They were walking with me, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with the son? Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I have no idea whether-- but we walked. And I understand, from-- from the book, it was only a mile. I thought it was like three, four miles, at least. But the town of Weimar is only a mile away out of Buchenwald, which is-- you know, it's no distance at all. We went to this beautiful little town of Weimar, and up to the railroad station and the cattle car-- well, this time there was open cars they're waiting, like a coal cars. They were waiting there for us. And we could see the beautiful streets, and the people, and the beautiful Germany scenery, and the houses, and everything else. They loaded us-- us on that train, and there began what was probably the most, without a doubt, the biggest hellish thing in-- in my life. It was three weeks. There were 3,000 people, including maybe about 500 Russian prisoners, that were on the row at the end of the train. And again, the young people, fortunately, were together. There was almost no food, no water. It was April. It was raining something terrible. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the train ride was three weeks long?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train ride was three-- normally, a train ride of that distance would be, at the most, I believe, like 24 hours, maybe 18 hours, or something like that. It was at least three weeks. Now, we wouldn't-- now, you have to remember, this is 19-- April of 1945. The war started-- finished in, I think, May. Germany is bombed out. The railroad system is bombed out. The train only traveled, maybe-- sometimes, we traveled five hours, sometimes six hours, sometimes we-- for days, we wouldn't travel at all. We just was standing on the rail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you get off the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got off the train. We were allowed to get off the train and stay-- and stay there. And they set up their machine guns a little bit further away, you know? This was the line you couldn't cross. And we were-- we were starving. We were just totally starving. People were dying left and right. And the-- the train next to us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with? Do you remember who you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- I was with a whole bunch of kids, you know, young people. Most of them were older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what-- what happened to your father or brother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had no idea. I had no idea, whatsoever. And on this train-- the next-- the train that was next to us, there was-- they just piled all the dead bodies. And it was literally full with dead skeletons, because that's what they were was skeletons. Sometimes, we would-- I think we-- we survived because, sometimes, they would-- we would go-- they let us go for water. And I was able to beg for food on the way. I-- German-- I would knock on German houses, and things like that, because I think I had to share whatever I got with the guards. Sometimes, the military, German military trains, would pass by close enough where we could see them, and stop them, and-- and beg them for something. And sometimes, I would get it. It was the first-- but was-- was such starvation that we-- we ate grass. And I took grass, and we were able to have little fires and-- and boil the grass. And it was-- all it was, it was just green water. I think some people probably ate flesh, human flesh. And I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I think so, with-- people were whispering, you know, they're eating flesh. But we had-- we were living-- like the young people were like in packs. Like I was part of a pack that, maybe, had 10, 12 members. And whatever food we're able to get, we would share it. We would cook it, and each one had the right to taste it. We had a taste. So I don't think we had many casualties among the young people. But you-- you know, you have to remember, we were in much better physical condition than the rest of the population. And I think what they-- the-- the death camps were overrun, so they didn't know what to do with us. I swear, they-- Anyhow, at the end of three weeks, we ended up in Theresienstadt, which is in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely not. I don't think they knew where they were taking us. I'd like to find some history of that, you know, some-- because there must be somebody that has written about it. I couldn't find it. We ended up in Theresienstadt. We're coming into Theresienstadt, and we had these Ukrainian guards. And they were son of a bitches. They were the worst. And Czech people come out offering us food, and flowers. And they-- and they tell us-- I think they told us that the-- the camp was under Red Cross supervision. The story goes that, what happened is that, I think, Hitler gave the order to destroy most of the inmates in the camps, so there would be no evidence. And he had orders to destroy Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Theresienstadt was a beautiful little village. OK? It was the model concentration camp where they brought the Red Cross and other people. And it was beautiful. It was run by the inmates. There was little houses. It was paradise, for us to get there. And apparently, the commandant of the-- of the Theresienstadt camp, he made a deal with the Red Cross. So when we-- the day that we arrived there, we were-- we were already-- the camp was free. OK? But they would not let us into the main-- the main town. They put us in a compound, because they were-- we were covered with lice. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're covered-- totally covered with lice. And so they-- they quarantined us for, like-- oh, that was the most horrible thing. Here we are. We're finally free, but we're quarantined. We've got a fence around us again. And-- but, you know, they knew what they were doing. So they disinfected us and everything else. And after like 10 or 12 days, they let us out to the camp. And by that time, the Russians were in the-- were in the camp. And we saw piles, lines and lines of soldiers-- German soldiers walking. It just-- I can end it up-- we-- I got terribly sick. I got terribly sick with typhus. Terrible-- the people died of typhus, like nothing. Terrible temperatures. They took me-- somebody took me, whatever, to their-- to a makeshift Russian hospital. I don't remember getting any medicine, or anything. I remember getting some food. And I think I was unconscious for days, and days, and days. And I was like, you know, on the borderline of-- I think I was dying. It was just a matter of one way or the other, going over that edge, because I know I was unconscious with very high fever. But somehow or other, I-- I survived that. And I was taken from there into a-- into a house where all the young people were housed. And I was, like, convalescing. And I heard my name, Sevek, which is my Polish name, being called. And I look out the window. And below me was my brother, in Theresienstadt, carrying a knapsack, German knapsack on his shoulder. And he had food, and things like that. And so, naturally, this is how we got reunited.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that moment like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was a very happy moment. I have-- I had a lot of feelings about that. It was a very happy moment to have my brother back. That was-- that was really terrific. And then, at that point, we were free, you know? But we were still in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the Jewish Refugee Committee in London, they sent in some people to organize the young people. They got-- the British government gave them permission to bring in 400 youngsters, but they all had to be under the age of 16. And they had it only for convalescent purposes, not to immigrate. OK? So it was-- so my brother was one of the organizers. All the young people lived in one-- one house. And he was one of the organizers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, the British government said, no, we won't allow anybody in under 13. So nobody had any birth certificate, or anything. And everybody looked so horrible anyway. So they made out papers. So everybody, if you were 16, you became 13. If you were 13, you became 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my next memory is that we went at-- we went to the Prague airport. And there was a lot of discussion with the Russian authorities about letting us leave. But we went to the Prague airport, and British Lancaster bombers were there. And that's the first time I ever heard English being spoken. These huge planes that I only seen-- seen since in the air were on the ground. And I just couldn't believe that anything that big could be-- go-- go up in the air.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- went onto those planes, and we flew into England. And we went to Windermere was the place. It was a very pleasant camp. And it was the northern part of England, in the mountain country.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we began our recuperation there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Mr. Finkel, you gave a very elaborate testimony. We-- we-- hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: [WHISPERS]

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERS) OK. You-- you came to England with-- with your brother. How long were you in England for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in England for five years, until I left for the United States, in 1950, in '51, I think. And the Jewish Refugee Committee took very good care of us. At first, we stayed in hostels with other youngsters that were liberated. And eventually, because I was younger, they sent me to school. They sent me to a boarding school called, Bunce Court. It was a very fine English school-- well, it was really a German school that was transplanted from Germany to England. But I had to totally learn everything, because I never-- well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Never really went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never went to school before, and-- but I'm talking about table manners, or eating. I-- to me-- I-- they made me sit next to the principal at meal time, because, to me, green vegetables were just grass. And I had a terrible time adjusting, terrible time adjusting to social thing-- I didn't know, really, much any manners, or any civilized conduct. I was still in a survival mode. But I did-- I did begin to learn to speak English and write English. And-- and I went to school. And I was doing pretty good, but it was very, very difficult to really get back, because I had missed a fundamental education. So then, here, I was 14 years old. I was already much more advanced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living with your brother? .

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was going to that boarding school. It was called Bunce Court. It was in the county of Kent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was in London. My brother was in London. He had found a job. He was working in a-- in a furniture making factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So how did you end up coming to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an uncle-- my-- my brother-- my father's youngest brother-- I think it was the youngest. Maybe he was the middle one. He came to America before World War I, so-- or-- yeah, I think so. No, after World War I. No, he had to come to America, probably, in the '20s. The story goes that, somehow or other, he was wanted by the police, maybe for illegal activities, or whatever. And his name is Alex Finkel. And he came to Chicago. So he and his daughter sent us a visa-- applied for a visa for me. And I came to this country in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. (WHISPERS) OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sitting next to me is my son, Leon, whom I'm very, very proud of. He's one of the great things in my life, for which I'm very grateful to have a son like him, and other children as well, who are not here. You want to say something, Leon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I grew up-- my dad's history was a mystery to me. And it wasn't until two years ago, at the Holocaust Museum, that I really learned about my dad's history. And it's opened up all kinds of doors to me and to my family, and also to my dad as well. Because now, in addition to talking here, he-- he talks to the children all over the state. I saw my dad last week talk at-- for the first time to a bunch of eighth graders and ninth graders and heard his story for the first time. And it was something that was very moving to me, that I was able to bring home and talk to my wife about, and someday, share with my children. And I can't tell him how important it is what he does for Jews, and for humanity, but also for me, and for my family, and for my sister, and for my sister's son, and for my half sister, and my stepsisters, as well, to know what my father went through and what other people like him went through, and to preserve that history for all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mr. Finkel, where we left off before, you were coming to America in 1951. But before we get there, I want to go back a little bit and ask you, did-- did you ever-- did you go back to your former town in Poland right after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have a cousin who did. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunt? Which-- which-- what-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel, the one that I've mentioned-- mentioned several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what happened with Rachel?

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived-- she survived the war. Her husband and her two sons, who I had last met in Buchenwald, did not. And she went back to her home town, together with two other people, and she was murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- what were the circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're not sure. Maybe, like she wanted to reclaim her home, and there were Polish people living in the house. And they were not very happy to see their owners come back for it, you know? I think the history says that the bandits killed her, but I doubt it. It was-- that was not that unusual. Recorded history of quite a few Jews who made it through this torture, finally are free, come back to their hometown and are murdered. I know a friend of mine narrowly escaped being murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back in Piotrkow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who murdered her? The Poles? Or was it-- was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Murdered by the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Murdered by the Poles. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted-- you didn't say that. I just wanted to establish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, murdered by the Christian Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the police, they were in uniform. They were the Polish police. You know, Ben [? Holcott ?] who is a very important person in London, he's a kind of a historian of our hometown. He narrowly writes about it in a book, which is the book that describes the pre-war and the after-war-- and the war years of Piotrkow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Did you ever-- did you ever find out about your father's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father died in Buchenwald, probably, about two days before I left Buchenwald. We had a death certificate. The Germans kept very good records. We have a-- we had a-- actually, a-- a death certificate of his death.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that after you-- you saw him for the last time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably, a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just never had occasion to see him after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to my knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a-- a-- a big regret of yours now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I would have liked to have been with him. Yes, now I would. But then, I didn't feel like I should.
